Competing Risks2026
Practicals

Author

Ronald Geskus

Published

May 5, 2006

Data

In the competing risks practical, we only use information on relapse and death. We investigate the role of the EBMT risk score, but we correct for age as a possible confounder. The EBMT score is a combination of donor-recipient match and age of the recipient, among other things. It divides patients into three risk groups. The event time and event status are in the columns srv and srvstat.

  • Load the R packages, mstate also loads the survival package. It contains the data for this practice
library(mstate)
library(ggsurvfit)
data(ebmt1)

1. A first look at the data

  • Have a look at the help file of the ebmt1 data set. Note that time is measured in days from transplantation, and the EBMT risk score is called score.
?ebmt1

  • Inspect the data by looking at the first couple of individuals or by viewing the complete data set. Summarize all the variables. How many relapses were observed and how many deaths? Why are there 1521 NA’s in the yrel variable?

  • Summary of the data

summary(ebmt1)
     patid           srv            srvstat            rel            relstat      
 Min.   :   1   Min.   :   0.5   Min.   :0.0000   Min.   :   0.5   Min.   :0.0000  
 1st Qu.: 495   1st Qu.: 157.0   1st Qu.:0.0000   1st Qu.: 113.0   1st Qu.:0.0000  
 Median : 989   Median : 692.0   Median :0.0000   Median : 395.0   Median :0.0000  
 Mean   : 989   Mean   : 854.5   Mean   :0.4411   Mean   : 701.3   Mean   :0.2307  
 3rd Qu.:1483   3rd Qu.:1400.0   3rd Qu.:1.0000   3rd Qu.:1127.0   3rd Qu.:0.0000  
 Max.   :1977   Max.   :3088.0   Max.   :1.0000   Max.   :3088.0   Max.   :1.0000  
        yrel           age                score     
 1993-1996: 103   Min.   : 0.00   Low risk   : 406  
 1997-1999: 208   1st Qu.:28.00   Medium risk:1404  
 2000-    : 145   Median :36.00   High risk  : 167  
 NA's     :1521   Mean   :35.81                     
                  3rd Qu.:45.00                     
                  Max.   :64.00
  • Number of relapses and deaths
table(ebmt1$srvstat)

   0    1 
1105  872 
table(ebmt1$relstat)

   0    1 
1521  456

There are 1521 missings (NA) in the variable yrel because these individuals did not have a relapse observed.

2. Estimation of relapse-free survival

Compute the Kaplan-Meier estimator for relapse-free survival. Give the estimate of the median relapse-free survival time. Obtain the estimates of at one and five years. Plot the survival curve with 95% confidence intervals and the “at risk” table.

  • Compute KM estimator and median survival time using survfit function. The Surv function creates a survival object for use as a response in a model formula. The result is a so-called survfit object, for which a number of functions (methods is the better word) are defined. If you just give the name of the object as command, the print method is invoked. It contains some very basic information on the relapse-free event-time curve, such as the median time.
KM.overall <- survfit(Surv(srv,srvstat)~1, data=ebmt1)
KM.overall # gives median value
Call: survfit(formula = Surv(srv, srvstat) ~ 1, data = ebmt1)

        n events median 0.95LCL 0.95UCL
[1,] 1977    872   2537    1828      NA
  • More detailed information is made visible using the summary function. It shows the event times, and the Kaplan-Meier estimate at those time points, and also other useful things like the numbers at risk, number of events etcetera. It has an argument times that we use to obtain relapse-free survival only at one and five years. If we want the time shown in years, we specify scale=365.25.
summary(KM.overall, times=c(1,5)*365.25)
Call: survfit(formula = Surv(srv, srvstat) ~ 1, data = ebmt1)

 time n.risk n.event survival std.err lower 95% CI upper 95% CI
  365   1199     698    0.640  0.0109        0.619        0.662
 1826    273     165    0.524  0.0126        0.500        0.550
  • Plot the estimate of the cumulative incidence with 95% confidence intervals. A survfit object also has a plot method, which plots a Kaplan-Meier survival curve. The fun argument is used to plot on the complementary scale of the cumulative incidence. The xscale argument allows to label time in years. The las argument rotates the labels along the y-axis.
plot(KM.overall, xlab="Time since transplant (yrs)", fun="event", xscale=365.25, las=1)
  • We also show the plot using the ggsurvfit in the R library ggsurvfit. It is straightforward to add an “at risk” table. We need to use ggplot2 functionality to label time in years.
ggsurvfit(KM.overall, type="risk") + 
  add_confidence_interval() + 
  add_risktable() + 
  scale_x_continuous("Time since transplant (yrs)", labels=1:8, breaks=365.25*(1:8))

3. Some data preparation for competing risks analyses

  • Add three more columns to the ebmt1 data set, time to first event, and type of first event as numeric and categorical variable. We also change the unit of time into years.
ebmt1 <- within(ebmt1,{
  time <- pmin(srv,rel)/365.25
  stat <-  ifelse(rel<srv,relstat,srvstat*2) #relapse=1, death=2
  type <- factor(stat, 
               labels=c("Event-free","Relapse","Death"))
})
head(ebmt1)
  patid  srv srvstat  rel relstat      yrel age       score       type stat      time
1     1 1610       0 1610       0      <NA>  28 Medium risk Event-free    0 4.4079398
2     2  961       1  165       1 1997-1999  33 Medium risk    Relapse    1 0.4517454
3     3 1508       0 1508       0      <NA>  38 Medium risk Event-free    0 4.1286790
4     4 1376       0 1376       0      <NA>  15 Medium risk Event-free    0 3.7672827
5     5 1585       0  133       1 1997-1999  26 Medium risk    Relapse    1 0.3641342
6     6  190       1   63       1 1997-1999  22 Medium risk    Relapse    1 0.1724846

4. Estimation of cause-specific cumulative incidence (Aalen-Johansen estimator)

  • Compute the Aalen-Johansen estimator of the cause-specific cumulative incicence and summarize the estimate at 1 and 5 years with 95% confidence intervals.
cuminc <- survfit(Surv(time,type)~1, ebmt1)
summ <- summary(cuminc, times=c(1,5))
summ
Call: survfit(formula = Surv(time, type) ~ 1, data = ebmt1)

 time n.risk n.event Pr((s0)) Pr(Relapse) Pr(Death)
    1   1012     897    0.538       0.156     0.306
    5    198     233    0.378       0.258     0.363
summ$lower
          (s0)   Relapse     Death
[1,] 0.5159829 0.1408540 0.2861585
[2,] 0.3547038 0.2373977 0.3415529
summ$upper
          (s0)   Relapse     Death
[1,] 0.5604699 0.1732993 0.3272103
[2,] 0.4036558 0.2810544 0.3864437

5. Some plots

  • Plot the estimates, with 95% confidence intervals, using the overlaid display format. Add a legend that links the curves to the two end points.
par(las=1) # labels along y-axis rotated in rest of R session
plot(cuminc, conf.int=TRUE, col = c(1,2))
legend("bottomright", legend = c("Relapse", "Death"), lwd = 1, col = c(1,2))

In ggsurvfit, the legend is generated automatically.

ggcuminc(cuminc, outcome=c("Relapse","Death")) + 
    add_confidence_interval() + theme(legend.position = "top")
  • Plot the estimates using the stacked display format, without confidence intervals. First plot the relapse-specific cumulative incidence, and plot the death-specific cumulative incidence on top of this curve. In order to use the correct range on the y-axis, we first plot the overall cumulative incidence, i.e. the estimate for both end points combined. Then we add the curve for relapse using the lines.survfit function.
KM.overall <- survfit(Surv(srv/365.25,srvstat)~1, data=ebmt1)
plot(KM.overall, lwd=2, lty=1, xaxs="i", conf.int=FALSE,
     fun="event", xlab="time since transplant")
lines(cuminc[2],  lwd=2, lty=2, conf.int=FALSE)
  text(c(6,6),c(0.1,0.4),c("relapse","death"))
  • Plot the estimates using the alternate display format, with 95% confidence intervals.
plot(cuminc[3], fun=\(x) 1-x, xlab="time since transplant", ylim=c(0,1))
lines(cuminc[2])
text(c(6,6),c(0.1,0.9),c("relapse","death"))

6. A Cox model for relapse-free survival.

  • Fit a proportional hazards model for relapse-free survival using the function coxph. Just like with the survfit function, the print function gives a short summary of the model fit, whereas the summary function gives more details. Again, if only the name of the object is specified, the print function is assumed.
PH.relsurv <- coxph(Surv(time, stat>0) ~ score, data = ebmt1)
PH.relsurv
Call:
coxph(formula = Surv(time, stat > 0) ~ score, data = ebmt1)

                   coef exp(coef) se(coef)     z        p
scoreMedium risk 0.5499    1.7331   0.0840 6.547 5.88e-11
scoreHigh risk   1.1330    3.1048   0.1172 9.668  < 2e-16

Likelihood ratio test=94.36  on 2 df, p=< 2.2e-16
n= 1977, number of events= 1141 
summary(PH.relsurv)
Call:
coxph(formula = Surv(time, stat > 0) ~ score, data = ebmt1)

  n= 1977, number of events= 1141 

                   coef exp(coef) se(coef)     z Pr(>|z|)    
scoreMedium risk 0.5499    1.7331   0.0840 6.547 5.88e-11 ***
scoreHigh risk   1.1330    3.1048   0.1172 9.668  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

                 exp(coef) exp(-coef) lower .95 upper .95
scoreMedium risk     1.733     0.5770     1.470     2.043
scoreHigh risk       3.105     0.3221     2.468     3.906

Concordance= 0.569  (se = 0.007 )
Likelihood ratio test= 94.36  on 2 df,   p=<2e-16
Wald test            = 94.36  on 2 df,   p=<2e-16
Score (logrank) test = 98.87  on 2 df,   p=<2e-16

The hazard ratios are in the column exp(coef). We see that those with medium and high value of the EBMT score have a significantly higher rate of losing the event-free survival status.

7. Regression on the cause-specific hazards

  • Fit a proportional hazards model for relapse and survival as competing risks. A proportional hazards model on the relapse-specific hazard can be fitted by censoring individuals when they experience the competing event. Only the individuals with stat equal to 1 had a relapse. Similarly for death: only individuals with stat equal to 2 died without relapse; individuals that had a relapse are censored.
fit.rel <- coxph(Surv(time,stat==1)~score, data=ebmt1)
fit.death <- coxph(Surv(time,stat==2)~score, data=ebmt1)
fit.rel
Call:
coxph(formula = Surv(time, stat == 1) ~ score, data = ebmt1)

                   coef exp(coef) se(coef)     z        p
scoreMedium risk 0.4319    1.5401   0.1255 3.440 0.000581
scoreHigh risk   1.0855    2.9609   0.1829 5.934 2.95e-09

Likelihood ratio test=33.03  on 2 df, p=6.73e-08
n= 1977, number of events= 456 
fit.death
Call:
coxph(formula = Surv(time, stat == 2) ~ score, data = ebmt1)

                   coef exp(coef) se(coef)     z        p
scoreMedium risk 0.6393    1.8951   0.1133 5.642 1.69e-08
scoreHigh risk   1.1782    3.2487   0.1534 7.680 1.59e-14

Likelihood ratio test=62.91  on 2 df, p=2.185e-14
n= 1977, number of events= 685

A higher value of EBMT risk score increases both cause-specific hazards in a fairly similar way. We would like to perform a test for the difference in hazard ratio for the two competing risks. For this we need to use a stacked data set.

8. Estimation of cause-specific cumulative incidence (PL-form)

  • Create the data set with censoring weights for the individuals who experienced the competing event, using either the finegray function in the survival package or the crprep function in the mstate package. Do this for both relapse and death as primary event. Include the variables age, score and type. Inspect the first ten rows of this data set with relapse as primary event.

In the crprep function, we can use either the stat or the type column to define the competing risks, and the data set with weights can be created in a single call. In the finegray function we need a separate call to the function for each competing risk. Also, only the type column is allowed; the variable needs to be a factor.

Webmt <- crprep(Tstop="time", status="stat", data=ebmt1, trans=c(1,2),
                cens=0, id="patid", keep=c("score","age","type"))
head(Webmt,10)
   patid    Tstart     Tstop status weight.cens       score age       type count failcode
1      1 0.0000000 4.4079398      0   1.0000000 Medium risk  28 Event-free     1        1
2      2 0.0000000 0.4517454      1   1.0000000 Medium risk  33    Relapse     1        1
3      3 0.0000000 4.1286790      0   1.0000000 Medium risk  38 Event-free     1        1
4      4 0.0000000 3.7672827      0   1.0000000 Medium risk  15 Event-free     1        1
5      5 0.0000000 0.3641342      1   1.0000000 Medium risk  26    Relapse     1        1
6      6 0.0000000 0.1724846      1   1.0000000 Medium risk  22    Relapse     1        1
7      7 0.0000000 2.3490760      0   1.0000000    Low risk   7 Event-free     1        1
8      8 0.0000000 0.1889117      2   1.0000000 Medium risk  44      Death     1        1
9      8 0.1889117 0.1943874      2   1.0000000 Medium risk  44      Death     2        1
10     8 0.1943874 0.2080767      2   0.9994019 Medium risk  44      Death     3        1
nrow(subset(Webmt,failcode==1))
[1] 94284
nrow(subset(Webmt,failcode==2))
[1] 33446

Apart from the Tstart, Tstop and status variables, a column weight.cens with the censoring weights has been created. If there had been left truncated data (specified via the Tstart argument in crprep), another column weight.trunc with the truncation weights would have been created. Two further columns have been added, count and failcode. failcode is used to separate the weighted data sets for the two types of end points. count gives the row number in the sequence of rows from the same individual. Its usefulness will become apparent in the exercises that follow.

Since there were no events of type 2 among the first 7 individuals, no extra extra follow-up was created for them. Individual number 8 remains in the risk set after the type-2 event (death) with a weight that changes over time. The second half of the data set is used for the analyses with death as event of interest. Here we see that individual 2 has extra follow-up after relapse, again with the weight changing over time.

Webmt.Relapse <- finegray(Surv(time,type)~., data=ebmt1, etype="Relapse")
Webmt.Death <- finegray(Surv(time,type)~., data=ebmt1, etype="Death")
head(Webmt.Relapse, 10)
   patid  srv srvstat  rel relstat      yrel age       score stat   fgstart    fgstop fgstatus
1      1 1610       0 1610       0      <NA>  28 Medium risk    0 0.0000000 4.4079398        0
2      2  961       1  165       1 1997-1999  33 Medium risk    1 0.0000000 0.4517454        1
3      3 1508       0 1508       0      <NA>  38 Medium risk    0 0.0000000 4.1286790        0
4      4 1376       0 1376       0      <NA>  15 Medium risk    0 0.0000000 3.7672827        0
5      5 1585       0  133       1 1997-1999  26 Medium risk    1 0.0000000 0.3641342        1
6      6  190       1   63       1 1997-1999  22 Medium risk    1 0.0000000 0.1724846        1
7      7  858       0  858       0      <NA>   7    Low risk    0 0.0000000 2.3490760        0
8      8   69       1   69       0      <NA>  44 Medium risk    2 0.0000000 0.1943874        0
9      8   69       1   69       0      <NA>  44 Medium risk    2 0.1943874 0.2108145        0
10     8   69       1   69       0      <NA>  44 Medium risk    2 0.2108145 0.2162902        0
        fgwt
1  1.0000000
2  1.0000000
3  1.0000000
4  1.0000000
5  1.0000000
6  1.0000000
7  1.0000000
8  1.0000000
9  0.9994019
10 0.9987940
nrow(Webmt.Relapse)
[1] 93844
nrow(Webmt.Death)
[1] 33184

The number of rows is slightly different from the number when crprep is used. However, it does not affect the estimates. The finegray function does not allow for left truncation weights.

  • Compute the PL-form of the estimator of the relapse-specific cumulative incidence. Compare the estimates and confidence intervals with the Aalen-Johansen estimator visually.
cuminc.crprep <- survfit(Surv(Tstart,Tstop,status==1)~1, data=Webmt, subset=failcode==1, weight=weight.cens)
cuminc.finegray <- survfit(Surv(fgstart,fgstop,fgstatus)~1, data=Webmt.Relapse, weight=fgwt)
plot(cuminc[2],lwd=2)
lines(cuminc.crprep, lwd=2, col="red", fun="event")
lines(cuminc.finegray, col="red", fun="event")

By looking at the numerical values, we can see that the estimates are the same. The 95% confidence intervals are slightly different. They are computed on the same log-scale. The difference is due to the different way in which the variance is calculated: based on the product-limit estimator instead of the Aalen-Johansen estimator.

summary(cuminc[2],times=c(1,5))$pstate
       Relapse
[1,] 0.1562367
[2,] 0.2583054
1-summary(cuminc.crprep,times=c(1,5))$surv
[1] 0.1562367 0.2583054
1-summary(cuminc.finegray,times=c(1,5))$surv
[1] 0.1562367 0.2583054
  • Compare the estimates with the relapse-free survival distribution, i.e. the distribution of time to remain free of both event types.

Both cause-specific cumulative incidences add up to the overall incidence that was calculated in the previous chapter. For example, after one year we have \(F_1(1)+F_2(1)=0.156+0.306=0.462=1-0.538=\overline{F}(1)\)

9. Impact of EBMT risk score on cause-specific cumulative incidence

  • Compute the data set with weights that are computed separately by value of score.

We obtain weights based on separate time-to-censoring distibutions per value of EBMT score by using strata="score" in the crprep function. We define the transitions by name, but we could have used numbers as well as we did in practical 8.

Webmt.score <- crprep(Tstop="time", status="type", data=ebmt1,
                      trans=c("Relapse","Death"), cens="Event-free", id="patid", strata="score")
## Webmt.score <- crprep(Tstop="time", status="stat", data=ebmt1,
##                       trans=c(1,2), cens=0, id="patid", strata="score")

In the finegray function we use + strata(score) on the right hand side of the formula.

Webmt.Relapse.score <- finegray(Surv(time,type)~.+strata(score), data=ebmt1, etype="Relapse")
Webmt.Death.score <- finegray(Surv(time,type)~.+strata(score), data=ebmt1, etype="Death")
  • Use the product-limit form to compute the cause-specific cumulative incidence by score for each of the competing risks using the score-specific weights. Compare results with the estimates using the same estimate of the time-to-censoring distribution for each value of EBMT score, as we did in practical 8.

We show results based on the finegray function, but crprep gives the same estimates.

## cuminc.relapse <- survfit(Surv(Tstart,Tstop,status=="Relapse")~score,
##   data=subset(Webmt.score,failcode=="Relapse"), weights=weight.cens)
## cuminc.death <- survfit(Surv(Tstart,Tstop,status=="Death")~score,
##                         data=subset(Webmt.score,failcode=="Death"), weights=weight.cens)
cuminc.relapse <- survfit(Surv(fgstart,fgstop,fgstatus)~score, data=Webmt.Relapse.score, weight=fgwt)
cuminc.death <- survfit(Surv(fgstart,fgstop,fgstatus)~score, data=Webmt.Death.score, weight=fgwt)
par(mfrow=c(1,2))
plot(cuminc.relapse, lwd=3,
  col=c("black","red","green"), fun="event", ylim=c(0,0.5))
lines(survfit(Surv(fgstart,fgstop,fgstatus)~score, data=Webmt.Relapse,
              weights=fgwt), lwd=1, col=c("black","red","green"), fun="event")
title("Relapse")
plot(cuminc.death, lwd=3,
  col=c("black","red","green"), fun="event", ylim=c(0,0.5))
lines(survfit(Surv(fgstart,fgstop,fgstatus)~score, data=Webmt.Death,
              weights=fgwt), lwd=1, col=c("black","red","green"), fun="event")
title("Death")
legend("bottomright", levels(Webmt$score), col=c("black","red","green"), lwd=3)

It is clear that the EBMT risk score has a profound influence on both relapse and death. Note that the low risk and medium risk curves cross for relapse as end point. This suggests some form of non-proportionality.

It is also seen that the estimates with one overall weight function gives almost the same estimates.

  • Estimate and plot the time to censoring distribution for each value of score. Does the distribution depend on the value of the EBMT risk score? Does this observation correspond to the amount of dependence on the weight function in the estimate of the crude risk?

We change the role of event and censoring. This is a classical survival setting, not a competing risks setting. Therefore, we can use standard code for survival analysis.

ggsurvfit(survfit2(Surv(time,stat==0)~score, data=ebmt1))

The time-to-censoring distribution is almost the same for the low and medium risk groups. For the high risk group, the censoring occurs earlier.

10. Log-rank tests

Run and compare the three commands given below. Why is the output from the first and the second different and what do these commands test. Why do the second and the third give the same value of the test statistic.

coxph(Surv(Tstart,Tstop,status=="Relapse")~score, data=
 subset(Webmt.score,failcode=="Relapse"), weights=weight.cens)$score
[1] 11.50557
coxph(Surv(Tstart,Tstop,status=="Relapse")~score,
        data=subset(Webmt.score,failcode=="Relapse"&count==1))$score
[1] 36.90983
survdiff(Surv(time,stat==1)~score,data=ebmt1)
Call:
survdiff(formula = Surv(time, stat == 1) ~ score, data = ebmt1)

                     N Observed Expected (O-E)^2/E (O-E)^2/V
score=Low risk     406       79    116.4    12.000     16.21
score=Medium risk 1404      328    315.0     0.539      1.75
score=High risk    167       49     24.7    24.023     25.54

 Chisq= 36.9  on 2 degrees of freedom, p= 1e-08

The first code performs a log-rank test with respect to the subdistribution hazards. Hence, it tests whether the relapse-specific cumulative incidences are equal for the three EBMT scores. The second and third perform a log-rank test on the cause-specific hazards. Hence, it is a nonparametric test for equality of the cause-specific hazards.

11. Impact of EBMT score on subdistribution hazards

  • Fit a proportional subdistribution hazards model for the impact of score on both relapse and death as first event. Compare results with those from the proportional cause-specific hazards model. Use the weights that do not depend on score1 (using score-specific weights hardly changes results).
fitsdh.rel <- coxph(Surv(Tstart,Tstop,status==1)~score,
                                      data=Webmt, subset=failcode==1, weights=weight.cens)
fitsdh.death <- coxph(Surv(Tstart,Tstop,status==2)~score,
                                      data=Webmt, subset=failcode==2, weights=weight.cens)
fitsdh.rel
Call:
coxph(formula = Surv(Tstart, Tstop, status == 1) ~ score, data = Webmt, 
    weights = weight.cens, subset = failcode == 1)

                   coef exp(coef) se(coef) robust se     z        p
scoreMedium risk 0.2401    1.2714   0.1253    0.1186 2.024 0.042961
scoreHigh risk   0.5712    1.7704   0.1819    0.1695 3.370 0.000751

Likelihood ratio test=9.64  on 2 df, p=0.008087
n= 94284, number of events= 456 
fitsdh.death
Call:
coxph(formula = Surv(Tstart, Tstop, status == 2) ~ score, data = Webmt, 
    weights = weight.cens, subset = failcode == 2)

                   coef exp(coef) se(coef) robust se     z        p
scoreMedium risk 0.5702    1.7687   0.1132    0.1114 5.121 3.04e-07
scoreHigh risk   0.9832    2.6730   0.1530    0.1460 6.734 1.65e-11

Likelihood ratio test=45.74  on 2 df, p=1.171e-10
n= 33446, number of events= 685
  • Calculate and plot cause-specific cumulative incidence curves. First create a data frame with six rows. Each row represents a combination of a value of risk score and event type. Next, we apply the function to compute the estimates.
indivs <- data.frame(score=levels(ebmt1$score))
par(mfrow=c(1,2),las=1)
pred.sdh <- survfit(fitsdh.rel, newdata=indivs)
plot(survfit(Surv(Tstart,Tstop,status=="Relapse")~score,
             data=subset(Webmt.score,failcode=="Relapse"), weights=weight.cens), lwd=1,
     col=c("black","red","green"), fun="event", ylim=c(0,0.5))
lines(pred.sdh,fun="event", col=c("black","red","green"), lwd=3)
title("Relapse")
pred.sdh <- survfit(fitsdh.death, newdata=indivs)
plot(survfit(Surv(Tstart,Tstop,status=="Death")~score,
             data=subset(Webmt.score,failcode=="Death"), weights=weight.cens), lwd=1,
             col=c("black","red","green"), fun="event", ylim=c(0,0.55))
lines(pred.sdh,fun="event", col=c("black","red","green"), lwd=3)
title("Death")
legend("bottomright", levels(ebmt1$score), col=c("black","red","green"), lwd=3 )

12. Regression on cause-specific hazard, combined analysis

  • Combine the effect of EBMT risk score on both events in one analysis. Check whether results coincide with the ones from the separate analyses.

We can create the stacked data set and add a column that specifies the type of end point as follows

tmp1 <- cbind(ebmt1,failcode=1)
tmp2 <- cbind(ebmt1,failcode=2)
ebmt.stack <- rbind(tmp1,tmp2)

However, if we use the data set created by the crprep function, then we already have such data set, and we can fit both a regression model for the cause-specific hazard as well as for the subdistribution hazrd.

For the cause-specific hazard, we restrict to the rows with count==1. These are the rows that represent the observed follow-up, and all weights have the value of one.

coxph(Surv(Tstop,status==failcode)~strata(failcode)*score, data=Webmt, subset=count==1)
Call:
coxph(formula = Surv(Tstop, status == failcode) ~ strata(failcode) * 
    score, data = Webmt, subset = count == 1)

                                               coef exp(coef) se(coef)     z        p
scoreMedium risk                            0.43186   1.54013  0.12553 3.440 0.000581
scoreHigh risk                              1.08549   2.96089  0.18292 5.934 2.95e-09
strata(failcode)failcode=2:scoreMedium risk 0.20743   1.23052  0.16911 1.227 0.219974
strata(failcode)failcode=2:scoreHigh risk   0.09275   1.09719  0.23873 0.389 0.697640

Likelihood ratio test=95.94  on 4 df, p=< 2.2e-16
n= 3954, number of events= 1141

Since EBMT score is a categorical variable, we can also perform the analysis via a compound score variable. We allow for separate baseline hazards for each end point, which are the hazards for the reference value of EBMT score (Low risk). Therefore, we only need one level for Low risk. We create four levels that give the contrasts of Medium risk and High risk for each of the two end points. An efficient, although maybe not very insightful way to create one compound score variable is via

Webmt$score.comb <- with(Webmt,
  factor( (as.numeric(score)-1)*(failcode==1)+
             3*(as.numeric(score)-1)*(failcode==2),
  labels=c("Low","Medium.Rel","High.Rel","Medium.Death","High.Death"))
  )

Fit the model via

coxph(Surv(Tstop,status==failcode)~strata(failcode)+ score.comb, data=Webmt, subset=count==1)
Call:
coxph(formula = Surv(Tstop, status == failcode) ~ strata(failcode) + 
    score.comb, data = Webmt, subset = count == 1)

                         coef exp(coef) se(coef)     z        p
score.combMedium.Rel   0.4319    1.5401   0.1255 3.440 0.000581
score.combHigh.Rel     1.0855    2.9609   0.1829 5.934 2.95e-09
score.combMedium.Death 0.6393    1.8951   0.1133 5.642 1.69e-08
score.combHigh.Death   1.1782    3.2487   0.1534 7.680 1.59e-14

Likelihood ratio test=95.94  on 4 df, p=< 2.2e-16
n= 3954, number of events= 1141

The parameter estimates and p-values are exactly equal to the results from the analyses per end point. The value of the likelihood ratio test and the first two parameter estimates are exactly equal to the specification with the interaction term, but the last two parameter estimates are different. The specification with the interaction gives the additional effect of medium and high risk for death as end point, relative to their effects on relapse: \(0.4319+0.2074=0.6393\) and \(1.0855+0.0927=1.1782\).

When fitting the model with the combined score variable, the values in score.comb are internally transformed into four columns with dummy variables. These are the type-specific variables. We can create them via

Webmt$Medium.Rel <- with(Webmt,
                              ifelse(score=="Medium risk"&failcode==1, 1, 0))
Webmt$High.Rel <- with(Webmt,
                            ifelse(score=="High risk"&failcode==1, 1, 0))
Webmt$Medium.Mort <- with(Webmt,
                               ifelse(score=="Medium risk"&failcode==2, 1, 0))
Webmt$High.Mort <- with(Webmt,
                             ifelse(score=="High risk"&failcode==2, 1, 0))

The model with these variables is fitted via

fit.csh.comb <- coxph(Surv(Tstop,status==failcode)~strata(failcode)+
                          Medium.Rel+High.Rel+Medium.Mort+High.Mort, data=Webmt, subset=count==1)
fit.csh.comb
Call:
coxph(formula = Surv(Tstop, status == failcode) ~ strata(failcode) + 
    Medium.Rel + High.Rel + Medium.Mort + High.Mort, data = Webmt, 
    subset = count == 1)

              coef exp(coef) se(coef)     z        p
Medium.Rel  0.4319    1.5401   0.1255 3.440 0.000581
High.Rel    1.0855    2.9609   0.1829 5.934 2.95e-09
Medium.Mort 0.6393    1.8951   0.1133 5.642 1.69e-08
High.Mort   1.1782    3.2487   0.1534 7.680 1.59e-14

Likelihood ratio test=95.94  on 4 df, p=< 2.2e-16
n= 3954, number of events= 1141

Apart from the naming of the variables, the output is the same as when the compound variable is used. We store this output in fit.csh.comb for later use.

13. Test for equality of effects

  • We already observed that the effects of Medium risk and High risk on both event types were comparable. We now formally test for equality.
coxph(Surv(Tstop,status==failcode)~strata(failcode)+score+Medium.Mort+High.Mort,
                                                  data=Webmt, subset=count==1)
Call:
coxph(formula = Surv(Tstop, status == failcode) ~ strata(failcode) + 
    score + Medium.Mort + High.Mort, data = Webmt, subset = count == 
    1)

                    coef exp(coef) se(coef)     z        p
scoreMedium risk 0.43186   1.54013  0.12553 3.440 0.000581
scoreHigh risk   1.08549   2.96089  0.18292 5.934 2.95e-09
Medium.Mort      0.20743   1.23052  0.16911 1.227 0.219974
High.Mort        0.09275   1.09719  0.23873 0.389 0.697640

Likelihood ratio test=95.94  on 4 df, p=< 2.2e-16
n= 3954, number of events= 1141

Footnotes

  1. Currently the finegray function has a bug in the calculation of covariate specific weights. It does not affect the PL-estimate, but it does affect the Fine and Gray model. See this issue↩︎
